priority-blind-load-shedding.label: Priority-blind load shedding
priority-blind-load-shedding.definition: Under overload the system drops work without regard to its importance, so shedding meant to protect the service also sacrifices its most critical traffic.

partial-completion-under-crashes.label: Partial completion under crashes
partial-completion-under-crashes.definition: A multi-step process interrupted between steps leaves its outcome and its side effects undetermined — some work done, some not, and no safe way to resume without either dropping or duplicating it.

single-table-scaling-ceiling.label: Single-table scaling ceiling
single-table-scaling-ceiling.definition: A single table's size or write volume outgrows what any one database instance can serve, and because a table is the smallest unit vertical partitioning can move, only splitting the table itself across shards relieves it.

ambiguous-failure-under-retry.label: Ambiguous failure under retry
ambiguous-failure-under-retry.definition: A request that times out or fails mid-flight leaves the caller unable to tell whether it already took effect, so a retry risks repeating an operation that in fact succeeded.

single-cluster-scaling-ceiling.label: Single-cluster scaling ceiling
single-cluster-scaling-ceiling.definition: A single database cluster becomes both a capacity ceiling and a shared-fate domain: it can only be scaled by ever-larger machines, and any incident on it degrades everything built on top — so relief means moving whole tables or domains onto separate clusters.

blast-radius-scales-with-cluster-size.label: Blast radius scales with cluster size
blast-radius-scales-with-cluster-size.definition: In one large shared cluster the cost of a single failure grows with node count — a lone slow or dead node degrades a disproportionate share of operations, and coordination overhead worsens with size — so the fix is smaller isolated cells.

buffer-degrades-under-backlog.label: Buffer degrades under backlog
buffer-degrades-under-backlog.definition: A component whose job is to absorb backlog is served by a substrate that slows down as the backlog grows, so the very condition it exists to handle erodes its ability to handle it.

gray-failure-defeats-automatic-detection.label: Gray failure defeats automatic detection
gray-failure-defeats-automatic-detection.definition: When a failure is partial and inconsistent, different components hold contradictory views of what is healthy, so automated detection cannot converge on a fault the system can't agree exists — and mitigation must fall to a human acting on ambiguous evidence.

observer-shares-fate-with-observed.label: Observer shares fate with observed
observer-shares-fate-with-observed.definition: A system depends on the very infrastructure it exists to watch or protect — most commonly monitoring running on the servers it monitors — so a failure in that foundation disables the safeguard exactly when it is needed.

retry-amplified-overload.label: Retry-amplified overload
retry-amplified-overload.definition: When failures are caused by overload, clients retrying to improve their own odds spend the struggling dependency's scarce capacity, multiplying load at the worst moment — and naive backoff does not fix it, because clients that failed together retry in sync.
